dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for breathing problems.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
My mom is having a really hard time breathing. She couldn't say more than a word at a time.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name?
Can you keep your eyes open and follow my finger with eye movements?
Can you help by telling me what month it is?
If you can squeeze my finger on 'three,' it will help me assess your motor response.
Ma'am, can I check the sound and clarity of your airway?
Do you mind if I have a look at how your chest rises and falls to see how you're breathing?
Pulse is 142 - fast and hard to feel.
Her pulse is 142, which is very rapid.
Respirations are 22, but see decreased chest rise and a significant use of neck muscles.
Initial BP didn't register - let's recheck after properly positioning the cuff.
Blood glucose reading is 122 mg/dl. It's normal for now.
SpO2 is at 91%. We need to assist with oxygen.
EKG shows atrial fibrillation. Let me keep an eye on this.
Ma'am, are you using your neck muscles to assist with your breathing?
Blood pressure is now 221 over 119. Significantly elevated.
Ma'am, can you tell us your medical history?
She has a long history of COPD, heart failure, hypertension, and was recently in the hospital for this.
On a scale from 1 to 10, how severe is your shortness of breath?
She was around sick kids yesterday and started having trouble breathing day before.
Has anyone tested for allergies today?
She is allergic to diltiazem; this was on her medication bottle.
Respirations just increased to 24.
Blood pressure is now 192 over 104. Still high, not improving.
I will be giving some inhaled medication including albuterol and ipratropium via nebulizer. This should open your airway.
I also will give dexamethasone to help reduce your airway inflammation.
Pulse is now 123 after medications.
Her breathing is now 26 per minute, still rapid.
I will initiate an IV. This is to provide fluids and give medications easily.
SpO2 has improved slightly to 99%.
After ten minutes, SpO2 remains at 99%.
EKG is still showing atrial fibrillation.
The rhythm still shows atrial fibrillation and needs to be watched closely.
I just placed more wires and need to continually monitor.
EKG lines are clean and showing consistent atrial fibrillation.
Rhythm remains unchanged temporarily.
Ma'am, we are going to place CPAP on you now to assist you with breathing.
Blood pressure is 221 over 129 again.
Breathing is 21, and appears less labored now.
SpO2 is at 100% now. Great improvement.
Her pulse is 141 again, a bit faster than previously.
Her breathing is slightly more shallow but still manageable at 18 per minute.
Blood pressure is now 202 over 126 - still elevated but less so.
Blood glucose remains at 0. Unable to get a reading.
Her SpO2 is at 100% and her EKG shows atrial fibrillation rhythm.
I will follow protocol 3-11 for respiratory distress with COPD.
Ma'am, we will be taking you to [COMPANY_NAME] for treatment.
Can you assist us in moving onto the stretcher at your comfort level?
You look comfortable now and the procedure is smooth.
ER has been notified. There is a known delay at triage, but patient is stable.
What medications is she currently taking on a regular basis?
Albuterol, Atorvastatin, Lasix, Metoprolol, [CITY], and something called fluticasone or vilanterol.
Oh, I think there is also something called fluticasone and another medication called Zyrtec in her list.
Also on lisinopril for her hypertension and she takes montelukast occasionally for breathing issues.
She uses prednisone occasionally when her symptoms worsen.
Has she used CPAP in the ICU settings recently?
Yes, she had a procedure done in the ICU with CPAP some days ago.
She is yet to require ETT procedures.
Let me recheck her BP quickly - at 202 over 126.
SpO2 is still 100%, which is great.
ER is receiving us now, I will brief them on your most recent status.
IV lines are intact, and breathing is being assisted effectively.
She will be moved gently from the stretcher to the ER bed.
All systems are operational and vitals are stable.
She has GERD and reflux in her medical history, in addition to COPD and heart failure.
All forms have been completed with patient information and medications.
We are reversing course and preparing to transport into the hospital.
Vitals are stable and will remain monitored during the move.
We're starting the hospital transfer now.
She is also on apixaban and dofetilide for heart rhythm and blood clot prevention.
She has a diagnosis of diabetes and is non-insulin dependent.
She has a history of osteoarthritis as noted in her medical record.
She takes a combination of fluticasone and vilanterol daily for long-term COPD management.
She is on spironolactone and furosemide for her heart failure and fluid balance.
She is also taking lisinopril and metoprolol for hypertension and heart failure.
She takes a regular dose of Zyrtec and is on docusate and senna for bowel function.
She has hyperlipidemia and is on atorvastatin.
Right now, her airway is clear and she is responding.
No signs of audible obstruction.
I will continue to monitor airway as per standing orders.
Her skin appears normal with no cyanosis observed.
Head and neck appear normal, no focal deficits observed.
No drainage from the nose and mouth, lips appear moist.
No visible oral lesions or signs of trauma.
I'll listen for breath sounds once CPAP is off momentarily.
Breathing sounds are reduced in all fields, especially on the left side.
Abdomen is soft and without tenderness or distention.
I just administered normal saline to maintain your hydration.
Noted allergies include diltiazem - please avoid if possible.
EKG - continuing to monitor and report changes.
Her pulse is now 146 - a noticeable rise from prior measurements.
EKG remains showing atrial fibrillation again.
At one point, SpO2 dropped to 0. That's now corrected with additional support.
I just increased FiO₂ and are maintaining CPAP at 10 cm H₂O.
We are also observing any need for change in medication under CPAP.
What's her breathing on a 1 to 10 scale right now?
She says it is getting a little better, maybe a 7 or 8.
EKG remains showing atrial fibrillation rhythm.
All steps from EPCR have been summarized in progress notes.
No issues encountered during transfer and she responded well to interventions.
She has experienced similar events in the past and was treated with CPAP.
No pain reported today during the examination or treatment despite underlying conditions.
Her extremities have good color and no signs of swelling.
No discoloration or rashes observed during the skin exams conducted.
We just resecured the IV portion and confirmed correct flow.
Emergency transportation is now ready to proceed. All systems go.
We are beginning hospital transfer now. ER is closing in on us.
She also takes dextroamphetamine for attention-related issues.
